subject: A European angle on your roof underwriting chart
body:
Hi SF Chronicle team,

(Writing to the SF Chronicle newsroom rather than Megan directly — we couldn't locate a direct contact. Feel free to forward if it's a better fit for them or the desk.)

Really enjoyed your piece compiling the roof underwriting rules across California's top 20 insurers. You did something most coverage skips: you went to the actual guidelines, not the talking points. The chart format makes the carrier consensus impossible to miss.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: what California carriers are now treating as a roof red line has been default Mediterranean practice for generations. The cultural lag between the two markets feels like the real story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on the Washington IBHS program
body:
Hi Insurance News Net team,

(Writing to the Insurance News Net newsroom directly — this is meant for whichever desk is closest to the topic below. Feel free to forward as appropriate.)

Really appreciated the piece on Washington's IBHS Wildfire Prepared Home rollout, particularly the detail on the three-year designation cycle and annual photo verification. That mitigation is treated as an ongoing relationship with the carrier, not a one-time stamp, is the part most coverage misses.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: what IBHS is now formalizing as a maintained designation has been default Mediterranean construction practice for generations, built in from day one rather than verified annually. The cultural gap feels like the real story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: Re: A European angle on the California insurance story
body:
Hello,

I know inboxes move fast — wanted to circle back in case the note from last week slipped through.

One data point that might be useful: insurance carriers still actively writing in California WUI zones are now underwriting concrete-shell homes significantly more favorably than wood frame, and that gap is becoming visible in renewal cycles.

Happy to share the underlying data if it's helpful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: Re: A European thought on the 100,000 FORTIFIED milestone
body:
Hello,

I realize the earlier note may have slipped through during a busy week — no worries at all.

One data point that's come up since: insurance carriers still writing in California WUI zip codes are underwriting concrete-shell homes meaningfully cleaner than wood frame, and the gap is now showing up in renewal cycles.

Thought that might be useful context alongside the FORTIFIED expansion, especially as resilience standards intersect with underwriting behavior. Happy to share the underlying data in case it's helpful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team

subject: A different idea of the resilient luxury home for the Westside
body:
Hello,

DIGS readers know the LA Westside and South Bay as well as anyone — which is why I thought of you. As insurability and fire-resilience quietly reshape what a luxury home even means here, the conversation around design is shifting too.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles — designed in exposed reinforced concrete, *cemento a vista*. In Europe, building for durability has been the default for generations; we pair it with the Italian sense of light, proportion, and indoor/outdoor flow.

I think there's a feature in how that combination reads on the Westside right now — a home that's both genuinely resilient and beautiful to live in. I'd be glad to offer a first look at a current build, with renders and photos, or a short talk with our founder, Paolo.

Would that be of interest to you?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on the Commissioner race
body:
Hi California team,

(Writing to the California newsroom directly — this is meant for whichever desk is closest to the topic below. Feel free to forward as appropriate.)

Really useful voter guide on the statewide races. You framed the Insurance Commissioner contest in the way that actually matters to homeowners: FAIR Plan reform and WUI insurability are what will decide whether people can rebuild at all.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your guide sparked: whoever wins, the resilience California is now codifying has been default Mediterranean practice for generations. That cultural lag feels like the deeper story behind the politics.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: the resilience now shaping insurability has been default Mediterranean practice for generations, long before it became a topic here. That cultural gap feels like the real story behind the asset question.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your Wyoming Company Uses High-Tech AI Sprinklers To Save Homes F piece
body:
Hi team,

Your piece on Wyoming's AI-driven sprinkler systems was a thoughtful look at how fire-resilient design is becoming a genuine priority for homeowners — the detail on real-time threat detection made the technology feel genuinely accessible.

My Villa is an LA-based developer building Italian-style homes in reinforced exposed concrete (cemento a vista), a material tradition that has quietly resisted fire and seismic stress across Mediterranean regions for generations — a timely parallel to the conversation your article opens up.

We would love to share more about our approach, and Paolo Mezzalama, our founder, would be glad to connect for a brief call if that is of interest.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

Really enjoyed your Carbon Beach piece. You did something most Malibu coverage avoids: putting real rebuild numbers on the page, the $10 to $18 million all-in above land, and what that actually buys today.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: at those budgets, the question is what makes a rebuild insurable and durable, and the resilience now demanded here has been default Mediterranean practice for generations. That cultural gap feels like the interesting story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli

info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your Who’s Running for California Insurance Commissioner? 2026 Pr piece
body:
Hi team,

Your 2026 primary guide on the California Insurance Commissioner race is a genuinely useful piece — the breakdown of where each candidate stands on wildfire risk and homeowner coverage gaps is exactly the kind of context readers need right now.

That policy conversation connects directly to what My Villa is doing on the ground. Founded by Paolo Mezzalama, My Villa builds Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced concrete — cemento a vista — a construction method rooted in Mediterranean building tradition that addresses the very resilience questions insurers and regulators are wrestling with. There is a real story here about how material choices intersect with insurability in California.

Would someone on your team be open to a brief conversation with Paolo, or to receiving more background?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: One thought after reading your FAIR Plan guide
body:
Hi Qualityspace team,

(Writing to the Qualityspace newsroom rather than Kevin directly — we couldn't locate a direct contact. Feel free to forward if it's a better fit for them or the desk.)

Really enjoyed your 2026 FAIR Plan guide. You explained the practical reality well: when brush, wildfire score, or roof condition push every admitted carrier away, the FAIR Plan becomes the only way to keep a home insured.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your guide sparked: the resilience that helps a home stay insurable has been default Mediterranean practice for generations, built into the structure rather than added later. That cultural gap feels like the real story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,

subject: A new kind of LA luxury home, built in exposed concrete
body:
Hello,

I follow The Real Deal's LA coverage closely — few outlets track how insurability and fire resilience are reshaping the high end of this market with as much precision.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles — designed in exposed reinforced concrete, *cemento a vista*. In Europe, resilience has been default construction practice for generations, long before California required it.

That feels relevant to your readers right now, as the question of what a "luxury home" even means in LA gets rewritten by what insurers will actually underwrite. There may be a feature in how design and durability stop being a trade-off.

I'd happily offer a first look at a current build — renders and photos, or a short talk with our founder, Paolo Mezzalama.

Would an angle like this fit what you're working on?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A new kind of LA luxury home, built in exposed concrete
body:
Hello,

Dirt has a sharp eye for how the meaning of a luxury home in LA keeps shifting — and right now, insurability and fire-resilience are quietly rewriting what "high-end" even means here.

That's exactly the space My Villa lives in. We bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles — designed in exposed reinforced concrete, *cemento a vista*. In Europe, building for durability and seismic performance has been the default for generations, long before California required it; we pair that with the Italian sense of light, proportion, and indoor/outdoor flow.

It might make a fresh feature for your readers: what luxury looks like when resilience is the starting point, not an upgrade. I'd be glad to offer a first look at a current build — renders and photos — or a short talk with our founder, Paolo Mezzalama.

Would an early look interest you?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team

subject: A European angle on your 2026 Election Results for California Insurance Commissioner  piece
body:
Hi team,

Your coverage of the California Insurance Commissioner race caught my attention — particularly the way you framed the stakes around homeowner access to coverage in a state where that conversation has become genuinely urgent.

My Villa builds Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced exposed concrete (cemento a vista), a Mediterranean construction tradition that speaks directly to the durability and fire-resistance questions now shaping California's insurance landscape. There's a longer cultural story here about how building practice and insurability are quietly connected.

subject: A European angle on your California chooses its next insurance watchdog amid wildfire piece
body:
Hi team,

Your piece on California's next insurance commissioner was a sharp look at how wildfire risk is reshaping the politics of coverage — the breakdown of each candidate's position on the FAIR Plan reforms was particularly useful.

My Villa builds Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced exposed concrete (cemento a vista), a material tradition rooted in Mediterranean climates where fire and seismic resilience have always been part of how people build. subject: A European angle on your Are Prefab and Modular Homes Finally Becoming Luxury? piece
body:
Hi Sara,

Your piece on prefab and modular homes crossing into the luxury tier was a genuinely thoughtful read — particularly the way you framed the tension between speed-to-build and the perception of craftsmanship. That distinction matters more than most coverage acknowledges.

My Villa is a Los Angeles-based company building Italian-designed homes in reinforced exposed concrete — cemento a vista — a method rooted in Mediterranean construction traditions where material honesty and structural permanence are considered luxury in themselves, not a compromise of it.

I think there's a natural conversation here. Would you be open to receiving some background materials, or perhaps a brief call with Paolo Mezzalama, our founder, to explore whether this fits anything you're working on?

Best,
Lisa Monelli

subject: A European angle on your Nearly 1 million Californians face home insurance spike in c piece
body:
Hi team,

Your recent Newsweek piece on the insurance rate spikes hitting nearly a million California homeowners was a sharp read — the granular detail on which ZIP codes are absorbing the steepest increases made the scale of the crisis genuinely tangible.

My Villa is a Los Angeles-based developer building Italian-designed homes in reinforced exposed concrete (cemento a vista), a material tradition that has quietly outlasted centuries of Mediterranean weather, seismic activity, and fire. Given how heavily insurers are now weighing construction type and fire resilience, that building philosophy feels more relevant than ever to the California conversation.

Would the team be open to receiving some background materials, or a short call with our founder Paolo Mezzalama?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team

subject: One thought after reading your LA insurance guide
body:
Hi Ownluxuryhomes team,

(Writing to the Ownluxuryhomes newsroom directly — this is meant for whichever desk is closest to the topic below. Feel free to forward as appropriate.)

Really useful guide on the LA luxury insurance crisis. You landed the detail most coverage glosses over: the FAIR Plan solvency strain, with exposure doubling to $650 billion, is now the pressure shaping what gets built and how.

A quick introduction: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and Italian livability to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete (cemento a vista). In Europe, resilience has been default construction practice for generations.

One angle that jumped out: what the admitted market is now demanding for insurability has been normal Mediterranean building practice long before insurability became a topic here. That lag feels like the interesting story.

If it sounds useful, would you like us to share more material, or set up a brief call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: One thought after reading your insurance voter guide
body:
Hi Laist team,

(Writing to the Laist newsroom rather than CalMatters directly — we couldn't locate a direct contact. Feel free to forward if it's a better fit for them or the desk.)

Really clear voter guide on the stakes of California's insurance commissioner race. You made the hard point most coverage softens: premiums are likely to rise whoever wins, so the lever shifts to the homes themselves.

A quick introduction: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and Italian livability to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete (cemento a vista). In Europe, resilience has been default construction practice for generations.

One angle that jumped out: while the politics decide pricing, what makes a home insurable in the first place has been normal Mediterranean building practice for a very long time. That cultural lag feels like the interesting story.

Curious to hear your thoughts. Would you be interested in a bit more background, or in a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

One angle that jumped out: as the market wrestles with where coverage sits, what makes a home insurable in the first place has been normal Mediterranean building practice long before any of this. That cultural lag feels like the interesting story.

subject: A European angle on your Can California boost wildfire prevention with less cash? A n piece
body:
Hi Noah,

Your piece on California's leaner approach to wildfire prevention was a genuinely thoughtful read — the tension between shrinking budgets and expanding risk came through clearly, and the policy framing felt measured rather than alarmist.

My Villa builds Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced exposed concrete — a material tradition rooted in Mediterranean climates where fire and seismic resilience have shaped how people build for centuries. There's a quiet conversation happening around whether construction itself can carry more of the mitigation burden, and Paolo Mezzalama, our founder, has been thinking seriously about that intersection.

If any of this feels relevant to a future story, we'd be glad to share more materials or set up a brief call with Paolo whenever the timing works for you.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your wildfire deductibles piece
body:
Hi Washington Post team,

(Writing to the Washington Post newsroom directly — this is meant for whichever desk is closest to the topic below. Feel free to forward as appropriate.)

Really enjoyed your investigation into California's wildfire deductibles. You landed the detail most coverage misses: that a single inquiry was enough to push the Commissioner toward a formal review, which says a lot about how unsettled these policies still are.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: the resilience California is now pricing into deductibles has been default Mediterranean building practice for generations. That cultural gap feels like the real story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your Client Challenge piece
body:
Hi team,

Your piece on the insurance crisis facing California homeowners hit on something most coverage glosses over — the structural vulnerability that quietly sits at the center of so many denied claims and dropped policies.

My Villa builds homes in Los Angeles using reinforced exposed concrete, a method rooted in Italian and broader Mediterranean construction tradition, where durability and material honesty have always been inseparable. That approach speaks directly to what your article surfaces: when the building itself is the risk, changing what you build with changes the conversation entirely.

Would you be open to receiving some background materials, or a short call with Paolo Mezzalama, our founder, to explore whether there's an angle worth developing together?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on the commissioner candidate statements
body:
Hi Voterguide team,

(Writing to the Voterguide newsroom directly — this is meant for whichever desk is closest to the topic below. Feel free to forward as appropriate.)

The candidate statements make for a revealing read — they show how many competing visions are now circling the same wildfire insurance crisis, from single-payer to market reform, without much consensus on what makes a home insurable.

A quick introduction: My Villa brings European construction resilience and Italian livability to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista. In Europe, resilience has been default construction practice for generations.

One thought worth following: whichever model wins, the resilience LA is debating has been normal Mediterranean practice for generations. That cultural lag feels like the real story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

Happy to share the underlying data if it's useful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli

subject: A European angle on the 2026 insurance crisis
body:
Hi Markets team,

(Writing to the Markets newsroom rather than Plentisoft directly — we couldn't locate a direct contact. Feel free to forward if it's a better fit for them or the desk.)

Really appreciated your rundown of what California homeowners face in 2026 — higher premiums, stricter underwriting, shrinking availability. You laid out the squeeze clearly without overpromising easy fixes.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: the durability that underwriters are now scrambling to price has been default Mediterranean building practice for generations. That cultural gap feels like the real story behind the crisis.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your Jane Kim leads CA insurance chief race, boasts $30K in real  piece
body:
Hi team,

Your piece on Jane Kim's insurance commissioner race caught our attention — particularly the thread running through it about how real estate money is quietly shaping the future of who can actually build and insure in California. It's a conversation that matters far beyond the ballot.

My Villa brings Italian-built homes to Los Angeles, constructed in reinforced concrete (cemento a vista) — a Mediterranean approach to permanence that speaks directly to why insurability and structural resilience are becoming inseparable topics here. The way Southern Europe has long built against fire and seismic risk feels newly relevant in this moment.

Would the team be open to a brief call with our founder, Paolo Mezzalama, or receiving some background materials?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: One thought on concrete and build speed in LA
body:
Hi Carbondaleconstruction team,

(Writing to the Carbondaleconstruction newsroom rather than admin3512597 directly — we couldn't locate a direct contact. Feel free to forward if it's a better fit for them or the desk.)

Really enjoyed your piece on ICF build speed versus wood frame — you challenged the assumption that stick framing is always the fastest path, which is a point most builders quietly skip.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: in Europe, building in concrete for resilience and durability has been the default for generations, long before anyone framed it as a speed-or-insurability trade-off. That cultural gap feels like the interesting story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli

subject: A European angle on your Who's running for insurance commissioner in CA's 2026 electi piece
body:
Hi team,

Your breakdown of the insurance commissioner candidates — and how each one is framing California's property coverage crisis — was a genuinely useful read. The way you mapped the policy positions against the underlying risk landscape made the stakes very clear.

My Villa builds Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced exposed concrete, a method rooted in Mediterranean construction culture that happens to address many of the resilience concerns driving today's insurance volatility. There is something worth noting in how a centuries-old building tradition quietly speaks to the durability questions California is now legislating around.

Would anyone on the team be open to a brief conversation with our founder, Paolo Mezzalama, or to receiving some background material?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

Happy to share the underlying data if it helps for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli

subject: A European angle on your LA Project Offers Blueprint for Resilient Housing Amid Wildf piece
body:
Hi team,

Your recent piece on fire-resistant housing in Los Angeles was a thoughtful and well-reported look at what resilient design could mean for communities facing real wildfire risk — the framing around scalable blueprints was particularly compelling.

My Villa is building Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced exposed concrete — cemento a vista — a material that has quietly shaped Mediterranean architecture for generations precisely because of its durability under extreme conditions. There is a natural thread connecting the fire-resilient future your article envisions to the way Southern European builders have long approached permanence.

We would love to share more about the project, or arrange a short conversation with our founder Paolo Mezzalama, if that feels useful.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: One thought on Commissioner Lara's decades-ahead framing
body:
Hi Insurance team,

(Writing to the Insurance newsroom directly — this is meant for whichever desk is closest to the topic below. Feel free to forward as appropriate.)

Really enjoyed your coverage of Commissioner Lara's oversight push, the 'decades ahead, not just today' framing is the part most reporting glosses over, and you gave it room to breathe.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista. In Europe, resilience has been default construction practice for generations.

One thought your piece sparked: the long-horizon resilience the Commissioner is asking for has been normal Mediterranean practice since long before insurability became a topic here. That cultural lag feels like the real story.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

Really enjoyed the coverage of AG Bonta's Affordability Response Team, connecting DOJ enforcement to the high-value home insurance squeeze is a thread most reporting leaves untouched.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought this sparked: behind the affordability fight sits a building question, and the resilient construction that keeps homes insurable has been default Mediterranean practice for generations, long before insurability became a topic here. That cultural gap feels like the real story.

subject: Re: A European angle on your Can California boost wildfire prevention with less cash? A n piece
body:
Hi team,

Apologies if my earlier note got buried — things move fast in a newsroom.

One figure that might be worth a line: the cost-per-square-foot delta between a concrete-envelope build and a standard Type V wood frame in WUI zones has held around $80–120 — large on paper, but increasingly recoverable through insurability and resale value. Happy to share the underlying data in case it's useful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your California FAIR Plan rates going up 29.1% in late 2026 – San piece
body:
Hi team,

Your piece on the California FAIR Plan's 29.1% rate increase landed at exactly the right moment — the way you laid out what that means for homeowners who've already lost access to standard market coverage made the stakes very clear.

My Villa builds Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced exposed concrete (cemento a vista), a construction method that has quietly shaped Mediterranean architecture for generations — and one that speaks directly to the durability and fire-resistance questions now driving California's insurance crisis.

Our founder Paolo Mezzalama would welcome a short conversation if this angle feels relevant to your ongoing coverage, or we're happy to send over materials first — whatever works best for you.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

  [IG] journal_companion: In California, insurance availability now shapes what gets built — not just what gets approved. Concrete construction has moved from preference to prerequisite for capital partners…
  [IG] journal_companion: After a non-renewal, most owners chase brokers. The smarter move is building the submission file first — because underwriters approve structures, not intentions. Link in bio. #MyVi…
  [IG] journal_companion: Rebuild permit data across Palisades, Altadena, and Malibu now lives in one tracker — and the pattern is clear: 2026 WUI Code compliance is where material decisions either protect …
  [IG] journal_companion: A $44M Malibu listing leads with its concrete construction — not its views. When structure becomes the headline feature, it tells you where coastal buyers have shifted their defini…
  [IG] journal_companion: Regulators can redraw who writes the policy. They cannot redraw what underwriters price first: the structure. That variable stays constant across every proposal on the 2026 ballot.…
  [IG] journal_companion: The candidate friendliest to real estate interests didn't make the runoff. Jane Kim leads the field for California Insurance Commissioner — and who wins in November will directly s…
  [IG] journal_companion: California's 2026 insurance laws don't just protect existing policies — they reward homes that make underwriters comfortable from the start. Build right, and coverage follows. Link…
  [IG] journal_companion: A Woolsey survivor turned the loss into a construction company. That pivot tells you more about where California building is headed than any market report. Link in bio. #MyVilla #M…

subject: Re: One thought after your wildfire prevention piece
body:
Hello,

Wanted to resend this in case my note from last week got buried.

One data point that may be worth flagging: insurance carriers still writing policies in California WUI ZIP codes are underwriting concrete-shell homes meaningfully cleaner than wood frame, and that gap is now showing up in renewal cycles — which may add a useful angle to ongoing wildfire-resilience coverage.

Happy to share the underlying data if it is useful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: Re: One thought on concrete and build speed in LA
body:
Hi team,

Apologies if my earlier note got buried — wanted to leave one more data point in case it's useful.

LA County permits for noncombustible residential construction have risen sharply since 2025, and the line is starting to bend. Given your focus on ICF build timelines, that shift in permit volume might be worth tracking as a follow-on angle. Happy to share the underlying data if it would be helpful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your Five new mansions hit market in elite Hidden Hills asking up piece
error: Address 'trd@therealdeal.com' is on the bounce blacklist (first bounced 2026-06-11T06:14:07+00:00): 550 5.1.1 The email account that you tried to reach does not exist. Please try double-checking the recipient's email address for typos or unnecessary spaces. For mo
body:
Hi team,

Your recent breakdown of the five new Hidden Hills listings was a sharp read — the way you contextualized price-per-square-foot against the neighborhood's gated, equestrian identity made the market dynamics genuinely clear.

My Villa is an LA-based developer building Italian-designed homes in reinforced exposed concrete — cemento a vista — bringing a Mediterranean sensibility to the city's luxury residential landscape. Given Hidden Hills' appetite for homes that feel architecturally considered rather than simply large, there's a natural conversation to be had around how Southern European building traditions translate into the LA market.

Would anyone on your team be open to receiving more material, or a brief call with Paolo Mezzalama, our founder?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: Re: A European angle on your LA Project Offers Blueprint for Resilient Housing Amid Wildf piece
body:
Hi team,

Wanted to make sure my note from last week didn't get buried. One figure that might be worth having: the cost-per-square-foot delta between a concrete-shell build and an unprotected wood-frame home in WUI ZIP codes is running roughly $80–120 — a gap that's increasingly recoverable through insurability and resale premiums in today's LA market.

Happy to share the underlying data in case it's useful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: Re: A European angle on your California FAIR Plan rates going up 29.1% in late 2026 – San piece
body:
Hi,

I wanted to resurface my note from last week in case it got buried.

One data point that may be worth flagging for your FAIR Plan coverage: insurance carriers still writing in California WUI ZIP codes are underwriting concrete-shell homes meaningfully cleaner than wood frame, and that gap is now showing up in renewal cycles — a tangible downstream effect of the rate pressure you've been reporting on.

Happy to share the underlying data if useful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle after your claims-process piece
body:
Hi Palisadian-Post team,

(Writing to the Palisadian-Post newsroom rather than Richard directly — we couldn't locate a direct contact. Feel free to forward if it's a better fit for them or the desk.)

Really enjoyed your first-person account of the claims process. You captured something most coverage skips: how much of the pain at claims time is shaped long before the fire, by how the home was built in the first place.

A quick hello from our side: at My Villa, we bring European construction resilience and the Italian way of living to homes in Los Angeles, designed in exposed reinforced concrete, cemento a vista.

One thought your piece sparked: what LA is now codifying for wildfire resilience has been default Mediterranean practice for generations. That cultural lag feels like the real story behind the claims math.

Would you be interested in receiving more material on what we are doing, or in setting up a short call with our founder Paolo?

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: A European angle on your Venezuela earthquake: Staggering destruction signals urgent  piece
body:
Hi Rong-Gong,

Your piece on the Venezuela earthquake and what it signals for California's building stock was genuinely sobering — the way you connected unreinforced masonry failures abroad to the vulnerabilities sitting quietly in California neighborhoods made the stakes feel very real and very local.

My Villa is doing something that speaks directly to that concern: Paolo Mezzalama is building Italian-designed homes in Los Angeles using reinforced concrete — cemento a vista — a structural approach with deep roots in Mediterranean architecture, where seismic resilience has shaped how people build for generations.

If this angle feels relevant to your ongoing earthquake-preparedness reporting, we would love to share more detail, or arrange a short conversation with Paolo whenever your schedule allows.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

subject: Re: One thought after your IBHS Serene Lakes report
body:
Just closing the loop on this thread. If a future story ever touches on how high-risk properties are being designed or positioned in mountain markets, Paolo Mezzalama would be glad to connect for a quick 30-minute conversation. The offer stands whenever the timing is right.

Best,
Lisa Monelli
My Villa Media Team
info@myvilla.la · myvilla.la

== PROPOSTE SOCIAL DA APPROVARE (87) ==
  • [IG] reactive  [img: alexepstein-author-alexepstein-tex-hero.jpg]
    Insurance is finally rewarding the right thing: a home built to reduce risk. As commentator Alex Epstein noted, California homeowners who invest in wildfire mitigation are starting to see lower premiums. That's how it should work — risk priced honestly. It's also the world we design for. A reinforce…
  • [IG] reactive  [img: perkykell-author-perkykell-text-d-hero.jpg]
    The FAIR Plan was meant to be a last resort. For too many in LA, it's become the only option. With an average ~30% rate increase now reshaping affordability for homeowners and buyers, the real question is how to build a home that admitted carriers will actually insure. The answer is structural: a no…
  • [X] reactive
    Insurers are now pricing risk-reduction directly: homeowners with wildfire mitigation are seeing lower premiums. This is the logic behind California's "Safer from Wildfires" 12/12 measures — and why we build to meet all 12 natively, with a non-combustible reinforced-concrete shell. #FireResilient #L…
  • [X] reactive
    California's FAIR Plan is raising rates ~30% on average. The path off it: build to admitted-carrier standards. A Type I non-combustible structure with a 4+ hour fire rating, meeting all 12 "Safer from Wildfires" measures, is how a home stays insurable in LA. #FAIRPlan #FireResilient #LosAngeles

body:
Hi team,

My note from last week may have gotten buried — no worries if so.

One data point that might be worth having on hand: insurance carriers still writing in California WUI ZIP codes are underwriting concrete-shell homes meaningfully cleaner than wood frame, and the gap is now showing up in renewal cycles — not just initial policy terms.

Happy to share the underlying data if it's useful for an upcoming piece.

Best,
Lisa Monelli

subject: My Villa · Social da rivedere: 20 contenuti — 28/06/2026
body:
MY VILLA — Abstract contenuti social del 28/06/2026
(replica di ciò che vedi nel pannello di controllo)

== PROPOSTE SOCIAL DA APPROVARE (9) ==
  • [IG] reactive  [img: hero.png]
    Insurance is becoming the real test of where you can live in LA. Stanford researchers found California homeowner premiums up 84% over six years — 45% even after inflation, with the steepest hikes in fire-country markets (per a Stanford study cited on X).
